speaker: interviewer
text: Hersch, and this is tape three.

speaker: interviewer
text: July 2nd.

speaker: interviewer
text: July 2nd, 1995. Confused. OK. So Peter, so this is still in Gusen II--

speaker: interviewee
text: Still in Gussen II.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and peeling potatoes. And you get another kapo who's just as bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, just as bad. And we were starving, of course, you know? We were hungry. There was nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me again. You said you got a bit of bread and a bit of soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that just once a day? And that was all you got?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then ersatz coffee, we got too. Ersatz cafe, you know, to drink. But whatever we could put in our stomachs, we tried.

speaker: interviewer
text: How often would you get the coffee? How often did you get coffee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't recall whether it was once a day or twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Breakfast or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had that. So of course, we were-- we were searching-- oh, I forgot to mention to you too, there, that'll come. So in Gusen, life is very, very hard, very tough. People were dying. When you went into the latrine, you know, to the toilets, because there was toilets. There was one latrine. And you saw just heaps stacked up of dead bodies, or in the corner of the camp. The bodies, we couldn't walk past without having dead bodies. And people that you knew, that you sort of befriended and talked to them. The next minute, they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a lot of people that didn't believe in anything, or didn't even want to say, especially a lot of the Hungarian Jews that came late, you know what they used to say? We didn't even know we were Jews. What are we doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they suffered more than anybody. Because they couldn't-- they, they they just couldn't take it. And they wouldn't take it. They-- they died like flies, a lot of them. And at night, we went to the-- to the, you know, like in the barracks to sleep. Well, one thing I learned from the other camps, is where to sleep, if you had a chance, is to grab the bunk up top. It was three levels. It was the bottom, then the middle, and the top. And if you slept on the bottom, excuse me, people urinated from up top on you. I mean, so or it collapsed. So you got the person from above you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I always tried to get on top. And another reason why is when the Germans used to come in, just for the fun of it, just bash you on the head while you were asleep. Kick you, do anything, or kill you while you were in bed, just for the fun of it. So upstairs, you had a better chance of protecting yourself. Out of reach.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I learned how to survive. And if we marched anywhere, always try to be not in the front and not in the back. But always in the middle. Because if you stayed back a little bit, you got shot. If you're in front, you could get shot, or on the sides. I tried to-- You see--

speaker: interviewer
text: Be in the middle.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is just, nobody teaches you these things. They're just instinct, how to survive. And I was determined even then to survive, and to, to, to get through this, you know? I made up my mind. I'm going to survive the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, I mean, for a 13 year old who had lost all their family and everything--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --what was it that you wanted to survive for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know. I was so annoyed, what they are doing to us that I wanted to, to go-- to, to survive. Because if you didn't, if you gave in, you were gone. I've seen that time and time again. Then my feet were frozed and swell-- swollen up like balloons. So they send you to the hospital there. Now, once you go to the hospital, you never come out of there. It was a hospital, just so but I had to go. They told me, you have got to go. They saw, you know, I couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have shoes in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know the Dutch, type of Dutch shoes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Clogs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clogs, yes. That's what we wore all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there in the hospital for a few days. This is also in Gusen II. And I noticed what was happening. I saw that as soon as somebody just didn't feel that good, they just chucked them through the window, you know, among the dead. So I said I've got to get out of here. I'm right. So I forced myself to walk straight and to-- unbelievable. I mean, those things come back to you. You don't think about it. So I went out of the hospital. As bad as the feet were, otherwise, I knew I was finished. I was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there two or three days. Which, of course, when you keep your feet up, the swelling goes down anyway. But I was determined not to stay because I was seeing what was happening there. And I got, got back out of the hospital. And they put me in-- I was at Block 13 once. Then put me in Block 2. Block 2, which was where they're marching. Or there was a Block 1 and 2 was sort of here. And this is where they assembled all the people to march or to work, and that. So all of a sudden, there was nothing for us for the time being. They put other people onto the potato peeling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, they always change. And all of a sudden, I noticed there was nothing. They didn't assign some of us younger ones to any work. When I say assign, they just tell you, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't like that. Then again, I thought to myself, this is not good because they're not going to just let you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to do nothing. So I made myself busy. Took a broom to sweep, to sweep there when the people went to work so that I'm noticed that I'm working. Because they wouldn't know whether I was assigned to it or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or not, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You understand? So in my head, I said, if we sit around-- that's when I came out of the hospital. If you sit around--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're dead.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you're dead. You just won't survive. And that's what I-- what I used to do for a while. You know? Till they assigned us afterwards to other work, you know? Whatever it was, to clean up the blocks, or to go out again onto the-- what do you call it-- tunnel.

speaker: interviewer
text: The mine?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was different things. It wasn't a set, set job. But I always made myself busy, that I survive. So then it came to April or March, March-- either March of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around about that time. All the Jews from camp Gusen assemble. See, up till then, we were together with all nationalities, you know? And of course, I didn't-- I didn't say the Polish kapos. There was one guy, Gustav. He used to be-- you know? I'm talking about in the camp itself. Besides the Germans, they had the kapos. And some Jewish kapos too, which were no good either. I'm not just saying that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were swearing at us in such filthy language, and bashing everybody he went past. God help you, if you were in his way. It was just terrible. So you were-- you were sort of-- every minute-- and people don't realize that every minute of every day, and every minute of every night, your life was in danger. You didn't know what was happening the next minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to, all the time-- firstly, you could hardly walk. You, you, you didn't-- you could hardly carry your body. And you have to think and, and scheme how to survive. So and this [NON-ENGLISH] day and night, all the time, all the time. And the worst thing was wintertime. And they made sure that you were completely naked after whenever they gave you the shot. And they sent you to stand there for hours. Naked in the snow for hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Naked in the snow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: For hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, it seemed like hours. It might have been an hour. But they counted. Then double counted. Then backwards and forwards, and standing there, while they're all dressed up, the, the Germans. So that was very hard. And the people that they killed in front of your eyes was just horrible. There was-- so we're finished with Gusen because you can go on and on and on. It happened every day of the time we were there. There were mountains of dead bodies all the time, stacked up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they not try and get rid of them?

words.text: try and get rid of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they tried. The more they get rid of, then another lot came. So they kept- keep- kept burning them as fast as they could. But it's just too-- too horrendous to describe the scenes there every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they still gassing? Did they gas people in Gusen as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know whether they were gassing people. But there were crematorium there, that I did see with my own eyes. A gas chamber, I don't think they had there. They had in Mauthausen, not in Gusen II. I'm back-- I'm in Gusen II.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they said, all the Jews from around Mauthausen, Gusen I, Gusen II were going to another camp. So we knew that there's already the end of it, the end because we heard-- of course, the bombers were coming over us, you know? The American bombers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they bombed Austria, like, probably Linz because it was near Linz. The town near us was Linz, near Gusen. And we could also see people. They say they don't know, the Germans haven't seen any. People were walking with their children on the road above the camp, walking up. And they could see the camps. They could see the crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: They knew what was going on.

words.text: They knew what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course they knew what was going on. And when the Allies were bombing, was air raids, we were put on a field so that they can see us, you know? When they were bombing, in the daytime, all everybody, all the ones from the camps, we had to get out and on a-- sort of on a big area so that the bombers, the people that were bombing, could see that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's probably where they had all that place that they worked on Messerschmitt, you know? So that they could see that they are-- So they wouldn't bomb that area?

speaker: interviewee
text: That they wouldn't bomb that area. So you know? Anyway, that's what happened with Gusen. Then we all assembled. And they were marching us to Gunskirchen. That's the last camp where I went to. And that was a nightmare. That was the death march. Because there we marched for days from Gusen, from everywhere. And there were only, only Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was this guy with me that we kept together in the camps. And his name is Braham Stern that lives here in Sydney. And I-- he couldn't walk anymore. So I begged him. I said, come. You have to come because they'll kill you here, you know? If we are all gone, and they find that-- Left?

speaker: interviewee
text: --and you knew because the Jews had a special marking, you know-- yellow, the yellow mark. And I said, you've got to come. You've got to come. I lifted him up, begging him to come. He said, that's it. I've had it. I can't move anymore. I can't go. Just leave me alone. And I left him there. And years later, I found him in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, really?

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was liberated before me. So anyway, then they took us on this march which is really was a death march. They were deliberately shooting us from front, from the back, from every other-- so that there'd be less people. And anybody that stayed behind a little bit, you know? And they couldn't walk so fast, they just shot him and just left him in the gutter. And this was-- I didn't know how far it was. But since then, I found out that it was about 30 kilometers, or 30 miles, I'm not sure which, from Gusen to Gunskirchen. And imagine people that can't--

speaker: interviewer
text: That weakened, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --walk, that can hardly drag themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get food and water on the walk? On the march?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got very little. I don't even remember getting much food. I can't even remember getting much food. But they must have given us, when we stopped, some, some something. And the thing that I remember very vividly, we went on the hill. It's called today-- it's called-- they gave it a name, and I didn't even know. The hill-- it's some special name they gave it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words-- I can't remember the name because there was a massacre there. When we got to that hill, we stayed overnight. We slept at night, you know? On the ground?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ground. On the ground, on an open field. And the shooting was all night long. But shooting at us, non-stop all night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were-- you can imagine the ones that-- we were dead tired after walking for miles and miles and miles. We slept always in the open. And in the morning, when they said, [GERMAN], you know? I pushed my friend next to me, that I knew, that we walked together, to get up. He was dead. He was shot. He'd been shot? What, were they just taking pot shots at the crowd?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just shooting like we were dogs, not even. So that was a terrible, terrible thing. In the morning, when you woke up, you got up. And you looked around you. And there was just dead bodies. I don't know what kind of people they are or they were. I just--

speaker: interviewer
text: These were the German guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the German guards, yeah. And they were not just SS. They were Wehrmacht. They were both. You know? Just for the-- the-- the-- the-- the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sport.

speaker: interviewee
text: --sport. And we went through towns. We begged for a piece of food, of water from the people. They saw us marching. And they must have known the end was coming. Nobody lifted a finger. Nobody. That's something that you don't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that march, it's a pity I can't think of it. But that was, like, a they named it. It's in the history-- Yeah, it's a death march.

speaker: interviewee
text: --books about that mountain, what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many-- So did you go through many towns? On the way?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through little towns. Yes, on the way because they were marching us to Gunskirchen. So we must have gone through lots of towns. I remember little towns, going through. There wasn't big cities. But certainly, towns and people were watching us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many? I mean, would, would people have-- wouldn't the guards have seen somebody giving you food or something like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, look, if carts would throw something at you, they'd know who it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: They would know who threw it.

words.text: They would know who threw it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And the guards were also being tired already. They were tired because they were walking with us. See, they were walking with their machine guns at us. So they were not walking fast anymore either. And they were older guards already too, you know? So we kept going and going. And what was happening on that march is just too horrendous to describe. The bashing, the beating, and the shooting. If anybody just stepped out of line a bit, if he was sort of weak, straight away, bang. And you see your friends being shot right in front of you. The people that you walk with. So this is what I said to you, I always try to get into the to the to the middle of it. [SIGHS] [SOBBING] That was very, very-- I can still see that march.

speaker: interviewer
text: You must have been one of the youngest in the-- amongst them too.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, we eventually got to Gunskirchen. And there, for the first time, I've seen men and women together. The women too, there. So they took the Jewish women from whatever camp they were in. And we were put-- there was no beds, nothing. It was just earth, ordinary ground, earth. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And no work. So we knew that is something is terrible going to happen to us because they're not just keeping you there for sitting, doing nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were on the ground. And there was very little food. There was one bread among 10 men. The men were still separate in the barracks. But some women there too. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember there was one man who he was the one, he was slicing the bread. And you can imagine all the eyes and all the faces. One slice of bread, that was it, for the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he a German?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, among us. They gave each 10 people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, and a knife? They got a knife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Yes. So there was already the end of the road. And we all knew already there's no way out. And it was in a sort of a forest, from what I remember. It was in a-- in a-- among trees. It was a-- it wasn't just a camp that you could see cars going past, or you could see people walking. It was just deep in a forest, the way I remember it. It may not have been, even, like that. And I remember the stories. We each told each other stories, just trying to keep the spirit up. And this guy, the one that was cutting the bread, he was talking what a good chef he was, [LAUGHS] and what he used to cook. [LAUGHS] You can imagine the things that one remembers. And we stayed there for quite a few weeks. I don't know how many weeks there was. It could have been-- it was in April. So it wouldn't have been more than four or five weeks, maybe. Maybe a bit more. I don't remember, exactly, a time or dates. We just didn't know. And then one day, it was Friday-- that date I do remember-- Friday, the 4th of May, 1945. A Jeep drove in, an American Jeep. And people started to-- we couldn't-- we were already that weak, that we had to hold each other to get up. You can imagine how we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then it's-- and an American Jeep came into the camp. And everybody says, American Jeep and American-- you know, American soldier. It was one Jeep just that drove in. And we saw the Germans throwing down their guns from the-- that surrounded us. And we knew that was the end of the war. And there was-- they had petrol all around the camp, surrounded, just to burn us all. And whether they waited for more people to come in or what to do the job in one hit, or before the Americans come in to the last minute. And it was just luck that he just drove in from-- apparently from the main road. He saw there was some-- some little road there. And he drove in. And he discovered the camp. And we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the Germans threw down-- I mean, there was one American soldier. Why didn't they just shoot him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they didn't know how many people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were coming with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: --were coming with him. He was the scout, obviously. But they saw, they knew that the Americans are there. They can't do anything, or he can-- you know? It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: It wouldn't look good if they killed him and the rest came with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, I suppose he didn't know. But that's what I remember. I mean, other Jeeps might have been behind. But I can only remember the one Jeep driving in that we saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we lifted each other up. And we held each other. And then we started to, you know, to say, we are free. And people started to cry, you know? And then we started to get out of the camp. You know? And we couldn't walk by ourselves. So we held each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, the Americans were very, very kind and very good. They start throwing chocolates [SOBBING] and food. And we started to eat like crazy because you know, we were so starving, so hungry. And of course, that's when a lot of people got sick and died like flies on the road. We went out on the main highway. And we just picked up everything they were throwing from the trucks. They could see the who we are.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were starving, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were very, very sick, all of us. But the first night, I remember, went up on a little, a little hill. And there was a house. And we sat down. The Germans, nobody was there. We sat outside. We made a fire. And the few that could still walk, and we sang [NON-ENGLISH], you know? And I still remember that. And then we went in to Wels, a place called Wels. That's the town next to Gunskirchen, was Wels, in Austria, which must not have been very far from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you told where to go, or did you just--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, people just walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your own accord, you just went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But then the Americans took us into this big, big camp in Wels, which is next to Gunskirchen, and deloused us because, of course, we were-- you can imagine. And they gave us showers and gave us food. And we became very sick, including me. I was very, very sick there. I don't know what I had. But I remember that they put wet towels on my face to try and get the temperature down. It's obviously from eating, you know, on a stomach that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your system couldn't cope.

speaker: interviewee
text: --couldn't cope, [BACKROUND NOISE] couldn't take. So we were there. And they wanted me to go to the hospital, the Americans, American soldiers. That came in, they wanted. And I wouldn't go to the hospital. I wouldn't go from the few people I knew with me. I mean, I'm on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were alone.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know? So I said, no way. And they looked after me. They, they, they kept me cool. And a few days later, I recuperated. I recovered from what I had. My hair fell out completely. I mean, I didn't have much hair. But I became bald. So I don't know what the heck I had. And they supplied us with food, of course, the American soldiers. And then they said, after a few weeks, they said, whoever wants to go back, the Czech government is sending buses to pick up their people from wherever we are, you know, to go back home. So of course, I said, naturally I'm going back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went on one of those-- I don't know whether it was a bus or a truck-- and made my way back home. When I said, back home, it's where I was born. To Loza, Irshava. Came to Irshava because there was a few people, a sprinkling of people arrived looking for if there's anybody alive. So that's when, I told you, I come back to the place. And when I got back home, our house was completely destroyed. There was no house. It was just flat, flattened. What they did was they destroyed a lot of the Jewish houses and looked for gold and diamonds, if anybody put in. But there was no house. It was completely destroyed. So I heard there's a transport in Bucharest. People were going to Bucharest because the, the Romanian Jews were still left there. And they were sort of saying that the transport should go there. And they can be looked after more by the Joint, and to Budapest from there and so on. But there was nobody in our place.

speaker: interviewee
text: I found some cousins. I found a cousin in Bilky. That's Malke's, my father's sister's son.

speaker: interviewer
text: The butcher?

speaker: interviewee
text: The butcher. Yes. And I also found another cousin in Irshava that I met. So we sort of were together. And I traveled then to Bucharest to see if I can find somebody from my family. I mean, I've got nothing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nowhere to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to do, nowhere to go. No papers, nothing. I just had a little piece of paper from Wels, from there-- I haven't got it anymore. I didn't keep it-- and no money. So we traveled on the top of the trains, or on the side of the train. You know, on the-- as you step, on a step. So I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Couldn't find anybody. There was nobody. And they say, do you know my sister? Do you know my brother? Do you know my anybody? If anybody had survived. And everybody was doing the same thing. So on the way back, I couldn't find anybody there. So on the way back, traveling on top of the train. And then there was no room on top of the train. You see, people, that's how we traveled then. So there was, you know, as you go on top of the train, like a tram-- tram step. So I was sitting on the step like this. And what happened was, a bridge was too close. And it cut into my foot. And this whole thing just stood up, as you can see. Oh, gosh.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was taken off the train and just left on the station. Nobody came near me. I couldn't speak the language. I didn't have money. And I was just--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sitting there, bleeding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sitting there, yes. So somehow they, they, they took me to a hospital. That was in Romania. I think it's called Brasov, a place. And they fixed, they stitched this. And of course, at least, I rested a bit. I had nowhere to go. But it was terrible. Because I couldn't communicate at all. So and they put you two this way in bed and two the other way. So four people in a bed. As you can imagine, that's what it was in 1945. Then from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's leave it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end of--
